chunk_1_background_facts.comment: Paragraph 15 - Background facts about the marriage and assets
chunk_1_background_facts.paragraph_text: The parties married in July 1995 after a courtship of four years. They have two children: a son aged 16 and a daughter aged 13. The husband is a property developer who built up a substantial business during the marriage, now valued at approximately £25M. The wife ceased work shortly after the birth of their first child and devoted herself full-time to homemaking and caring for the children. The matrimonial home in Kensington is valued at £30M. The husband also has business premises valued at £5M and investment properties generating £800k per annum. The wife has made no direct financial contributions but has been the primary caregiver throughout the 20-year marriage.
chunk_1_background_facts.enriched_text: [OVERLAP FROM PARA 14] The husband filed his Form E on 15 January 2016 disclosing total assets of £55M. The wife's Form E disclosed assets of £2M consisting mainly of jewelry and personal chattels. [END OVERLAP] The parties married in July 1995 after a courtship of four years. They have two children: a son aged 16 and a daughter aged 13. The husband is a property developer who built up a substantial business during the marriage, now valued at approximately £25M. The wife ceased work shortly after the birth of their first child and devoted herself full-time to homemaking and caring for the children. The matrimonial home in Kensington is valued at £30M. The husband also has business premises valued at £5M and investment properties generating £800k per annum. The wife has made no direct financial contributions but has been the primary caregiver throughout the 20-year marriage.

people.description: primary homemaker, ceased work after first child

chunk_1_background_facts.reasoning: Critical factual background establishing marriage duration, roles, and asset accumulation
chunk_1_background_facts.semantic_summary: Background facts paragraph establishing key matrimonial circumstances. 20-year marriage (1995-present) with two children aged 16 and 13. Husband property developer who built £25M business during marriage plus matrimonial home worth £30M and investment properties generating £800k annually. Wife ceased work after first child's birth and was primary caregiver throughout marriage. Classic scenario of homemaker wife versus business-building husband in long marriage with substantial assets (£55M total) accumulated during marriage. Wife made no direct financial contributions but significant homemaker and childcare contributions. Marriage duration and wife's role as primary caregiver are critical factors for Section 25(2)(f) analysis.

legal_principles_identified.principle: Homemaker contributions equal to financial contributions in long marriage
Business built during marriage is matrimonial property

distinguishing_features: Clear role division: breadwinner vs homemaker

case_context.comment: This object is SHARED by ALL paragraphs in this case - ensures consistency

chunk_2_ratio_decidendi.comment: Paragraph 87 - Court's reasoning and order (ratio decidendi)
chunk_2_ratio_decidendi.paragraph_text: Applying the principles in White v White and Miller v Miller, I find that this is a paradigm case of equal sharing. The marriage lasted 20 years. The wife's contributions as homemaker and primary caregiver were equal in value to the husband's financial contributions. The business, though built by the husband's skill and endeavor, was accumulated entirely during the marriage. The wife's role enabled the husband to focus on building the business. I reject the husband's argument for a 60/40 split in his favor. This would fail to recognize the equal value of non-financial contributions in a long marriage. Accordingly, I order that the assets be divided equally: the wife shall receive the matrimonial home (£30M), half the business value (£12.5M), and sufficient liquid assets to bring her total to £27.5M. The husband retains the business and investment properties. This achieves an equal division and meets the wife's needs while allowing the husband to continue operating the business.
chunk_2_ratio_decidendi.enriched_text: [OVERLAP FROM PARA 86] The husband submits that he should retain 60% of the assets based on his business acumen and entrepreneurial skill. The wife seeks equality based on her homemaker contributions during the 20-year marriage. [END OVERLAP] Applying the principles in White v White and Miller v Miller, I find that this is a paradigm case of equal sharing. The marriage lasted 20 years. The wife's contributions as homemaker and primary caregiver were equal in value to the husband's financial contributions. The business, though built by the husband's skill and endeavor, was accumulated entirely during the marriage. The wife's role enabled the husband to focus on building the business. I reject the husband's argument for a 60/40 split in his favor. This would fail to recognize the equal value of non-financial contributions in a long marriage. Accordingly, I order that the assets be divided equally: the wife shall receive the matrimonial home (£30M), half the business value (£12.5M), and sufficient liquid assets to bring her total to £27.5M. The husband retains the business and investment properties. This achieves an equal division and meets the wife's needs while allowing the husband to continue operating the business.

people.receives: matrimonial home + £12.5M business value + liquid assets = £27.5M total

chunk_2_ratio_decidendi.reasoning: Core legal reasoning and final order - this is the binding precedent
chunk_2_ratio_decidendi.semantic_summary: Ratio decidendi paragraph containing court's binding legal reasoning and final order. Judge applies White v White and Miller v Miller principles to order equal (50/50) division of £55M assets. Explicitly rejects husband's 60/40 argument. Finds wife's homemaker contributions in 20-year marriage equal in value to husband's financial contributions. Though business built by husband's skill, it was accumulated during marriage while wife enabled his focus through homemaking. Wife awarded matrimonial home (£30M) plus £12.5M business value plus liquid assets totaling £27.5M. Husband retains business and investment properties totaling £27.5M. Order achieves equal sharing while meeting wife's needs and allowing husband to continue business operations. Critical precedent establishing equal sharing principle for long marriage with homemaker wife and business-building husband.

legal_principles_identified.principle: Equal sharing is paradigm outcome in long marriage
Homemaker contributions equal in value to financial contributions
Business built during marriage is matrimonial property despite one party's skill
Homemaker role enables breadwinner to build business
